description:
Week-1 of the HCL Java CDC program covers Frontend Development.

Duration: ~30 Hours total

Sections:
1. JavaScript (JS) — 12 Hours (Mentor-led)
2. React JS (Frontend Framework) — 18 Hours (Mentor-led)
3. Mock Assessment

Goal: Build a working frontend with ReactJS, consume REST APIs, handle routing and state management.

Mode: ILT with Hands-on
Ownership: College
Faculty: Dr NIVETHITHA V — nivethitha.v@vit.ac.in

Mentor-led session — Duration: 12 Hours

JavaScript is the language of the web. Every browser understands JS natively.

Why it matters for HCL:
• Spring Boot backend serves REST APIs — JS frontend consumes them
• ReactJS is built on JS — you must know JS before React
• Modern JS (ES6+) is used in all production codebases

Topics covered:
1. Basics — Syntax, Variables, Operators
2. DOM Manipulation and Events
3. ES6+ Features (Arrow Functions, Promises, Modules)
4. Fetch API and Asynchronous Programming
5. Introduction to JavaScript Frameworks and Libraries

Status: Week-1 content

SYNTAX & VARIABLES:

var vs let vs const:
• var — function-scoped, hoisted, avoid in modern code
• let — block-scoped, reassignable
• const — block-scoped, cannot be reassigned (but object properties can change)

Data types:
• Primitive: string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, BigInt
• Reference: object, array, function

Type coercion:
• == does type coercion: '5' == 5 → true
• === strict equality: '5' === 5 → false (always use ===)

OPERATORS:
• Arithmetic: + - * / % **
• Comparison: === !== < > <= >=
• Logical: && || ! (short-circuit evaluation)
• Nullish coalescing: ?? (returns right side only if left is null/undefined)
• Optional chaining: obj?.property (safe property access)

KEY CONCEPTS:
• Hoisting: var declarations moved to top of scope at compile time
• Temporal Dead Zone: let/const cannot be accessed before declaration line
• Truthy/Falsy: 0, '', null, undefined, NaN, false are falsy; everything else is truthy

EXAMPLES:
  const name = 'HCL';
  let count = 0;
  count++;
  const result = count > 0 ? 'positive' : 'zero';
  const val = null ?? 'default';  // 'default'
  const len = obj?.name?.length;  // safe access

text: Introduction to JavaScript Frameworks and Libraries
description:
WHY FRAMEWORKS?
Pure JS DOM manipulation becomes unmaintainable at scale. Frameworks provide:
• Component-based UI architecture
• Reactive state management
• Routing, build tooling, testing

MAJOR JS FRAMEWORKS:

• React (Meta) — Component library, virtual DOM, most popular
  Used by: Facebook, Airbnb, Netflix, Uber
  Learn: react.dev

• Angular (Google) — Full MVC framework, TypeScript-first
  Used by: Google, Microsoft, Deutsche Bank
  Learn: angular.dev

• Vue.js (Community) — Progressive framework, gentle learning curve
  Used by: Alibaba, Xiaomi, GitLab
  Learn: vuejs.org

• Svelte — Compiler-based, no virtual DOM, tiny bundle size

KEY CONCEPTS SHARED BY ALL:
• Component: reusable, self-contained UI piece (header, button, form)
• State: data that when changed, re-renders the UI
• Props: data passed from parent to child component
• Lifecycle: component mount → update → unmount
• Routing: client-side URL-to-component mapping

WHY REACT FOR HCL?
• Most popular (65% of JS developer usage)
• Huge ecosystem (Next.js, Redux, React Query)
• Pairs naturally with Spring Boot REST APIs
• Industry standard in enterprise Java shops

BUILD TOOLS:
• Vite — fast dev server and bundler (used in this project)
• Webpack — older, highly configurable bundler
• npm / Yarn — package managers

Introduction to ReactJS and JSX — Mentor-led session, Duration: 18 Hours React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces using components. Created by Facebook/Meta (2013). Powers Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp Web. CORE IDEA: UI = f(state) — given the same state, the UI is always the same VIRTUAL DOM: React keeps a lightweight copy of the DOM in memory. On state change → diff old vs new virtual DOM → update only changed real DOM nodes. This is called reconciliation. JSX: Syntactic sugar for React.createElement(). Babel compiles it. const el =

Hello {name}

; // Compiles to: const el = React.createElement('h1', {className:'title'}, 'Hello ' + name); RULES OF JSX: • className instead of class • htmlFor instead of for • All tags must be closed:
• Return single root element (or Fragment: <>...) • JS expressions in {curly braces}: {2 + 2}, {name}, {condition ? 'a' : 'b'} Topics in this section: 1. Components, Props, and State 2. React Lifecycle Methods and Hooks 3. State Management (Context API, Redux) 4. Routing with React Router 5. Integrating APIs with Axios or Fetch 6. Advanced Topics (Performance Optimization, Error Boundaries) 7. Mini Project Development

COMPONENTS: Function components are the modern standard (class components are legacy). // Function component function Greeting({ name, age }) { return

Hello {name}, age {age}

; } // Usage PROPS: • Props = properties passed from parent to child (read-only in child) • Can be any type: string, number, boolean, object, array, function • Destructure in function signature: function Card({ title, onClick }) • children prop: whatever is between opening and closing tags

Content

 → props.children =

Content

STATE (useState): import { useState } from 'react'; function Counter() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); // [value, setter] return ( <>

{count}

); } KEY RULES: • Never mutate state directly: setItems([...items, newItem]) not items.push() • State updates are async — don't read state immediately after set • For object state: spread the old state: setUser({...user, name: 'Bob'}) COMPONENT TREE: App ├── Header ├── Main │ ├── Sidebar │ └── Content │ ├── Card │ └── Card └── Footer Data flows DOWN (props). Events flow UP (callback functions passed as props).

text: Integrating APIs with Axios or Fetch

description:
Build a complete ReactJS frontend as a mini project.

SUGGESTED PROJECT IDEAS (from HCL CDC):

1. MealDB App
   • Fetch meals from https://www.themealdb.com/api/json/v1/1/search.php?s=
   • Display meals, search by name, show meal details
   • Find meal with fewest ingredients

2. Rest Countries App
   • Use REST Countries API
   • List all countries, search by name/code/continent/capital
   • Show country details (flag, population, capital)

3. Spotify Clone (simplified)
   • Song search, playlist management
   • Requires Spotify Developer API key

4. Freshdesk Clone
   • Ticket CRUD operations
   • Contact management
   • Use Freshdesk sandbox API

PROJECT CHECKLIST:
□ Create React app with Vite
□ Set up React Router for multiple pages
□ Create reusable components (Card, Button, Modal, Loader)
□ Implement API calls with Axios
□ Handle loading, error, and empty states
□ Add basic form with validation
□ Use useState and useEffect correctly
□ Style with CSS modules or Tailwind
□ Deploy to Netlify or Vercel

FOLDER STRUCTURE:
  src/
  ├── components/   (reusable UI)
  ├── pages/        (route-level components)
  ├── services/     (API calls)
  ├── hooks/        (custom hooks)
  └── utils/        (helper functions)

Frontend Mock Assessment after Week-1.

Expected coverage:
• JavaScript fundamentals (variables, closures, this, event loop)
• DOM manipulation and event handling
• ES6+ syntax (arrow functions, destructuring, spread, async/await)
• Fetch API usage
• React components, props, state
• useEffect, useState hooks
• Simple routing with React Router

FORMAT (typical):
• MCQ on JS/React concepts
• Code snippet analysis (what is the output?)
• Write a React component from requirements
• Debug a given React component

PREP TIPS:
• Practice writing components from scratch without IDE help
• Understand the Event Loop — common MCQ topic
• Know the difference between == and ===, var/let/const
• Understand React re-render triggers
• Be able to implement a basic CRUD UI with React

Weeks 2–3 of the HCL Java CDC program cover Backend Development.

Sections:
1. Core Java Programming — Refresher (12 Hours, Week-2)
2. Java 8 & Latest — (14 Hours, Week-2)
3. Spring IOC & Beans — (16 Hours, Week-2/3)

Mode: Instructor-led (ILT) with Hands-on
Ownership: College

Faculty:
• Core Java: Dr Rajiv Vincent — rajiv.vincent@vit.ac.in
• Java 8: Dr. RAJESH M — rajesh.m@vit.ac.in
• Spring: Dr THOMAS ABRAHAM J V — thomasabraham.jv@vit.ac.in

Goal: Build production-quality REST APIs using Spring Boot, handle data with JPA, secure with Spring Security & JWT.

Instructor-led session — Duration: 12 Hours (Week-2)
Faculty: Dr Rajiv Vincent

This is a refresher for Java fundamentals. Since you already know Java (Spring Boot background), use this to fill gaps and ensure production-level understanding.

Topics:
1. Intro to Java Platform & Language
2. JVM, JRE, JDK
3. JVM Architecture
4. Data types — Primitive, Arrays
5. Operators
6. Branching (if, switch)
7. Looping (while, for)
8. OOPs in Java
9. Classes, fields, methods, constructors
10. Keywords this, super & Modifiers
11. Interfaces & Inheritance
12. Method overloading & Overriding
13. Abstract classes
14. Packages
15. Access modifiers
16. Exception Handling
17. Collections (Comparable & Comparator)
18. File Handling
19. Memory management & Garbage collection
20. Collections Framework (List, Set, Map, Queue)
21. Generics

JAVA PLATFORM:
• Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA) — Java bytecode runs on any JVM
• Compiled to bytecode (.class), not native machine code
• JVM interprets/JIT-compiles bytecode at runtime

JDK vs JRE vs JVM:
• JDK = Java Development Kit — compiler (javac), debugger, tools + JRE
• JRE = Java Runtime Environment — JVM + standard libraries
• JVM = Java Virtual Machine — executes bytecode

JVM, JRE, JDK:
  JDK
  └── JRE
      └── JVM (Class Loader, Execution Engine, Runtime Data Areas)

JVM ARCHITECTURE:
  Class Loader — loads .class files
  Runtime Data Areas:
    • Method Area — class-level data (static vars, method bytecode)
    • Heap — objects and arrays (GC manages this)
    • Stack — one per thread, holds stack frames (local vars, method calls)
    • PC Register — current instruction pointer per thread
    • Native Method Stack — for native (C/C++) methods
  Execution Engine:
    • Interpreter — executes bytecode line by line
    • JIT Compiler — compiles hot code paths to native machine code
    • Garbage Collector — automatic memory management

JAVA VERSIONS (LTS):
  Java 8 (2014)  — Lambdas, Streams, Optional (still widely used)
  Java 11 (2018) — LTS, HTTP Client, String methods
  Java 17 (2021) — LTS, Records, Sealed classes, Pattern matching
  Java 21 (2023) — LTS, Virtual Threads (Project Loom), Pattern matching++

HCL uses Java 8–17 range. Know Java 8 features thoroughly.

Instructor-led session — Duration: 14 Hours (Week-2)
Faculty: Dr. RAJESH M

Java 8 (2014) was the most significant Java release in a decade.
It introduced functional programming to Java.

Key Java 8 features:
1. Lambda expressions
2. Functional interfaces
3. Method references
4. Optional class
5. Streams API
6. Date & Time API (java.time)
7. Callable & Future interfaces
8. CompletableFuture & CompletionStage

Java 9–17 additions:
9. Brief intro to Java 9-17 features
10. Collectors API & Immutable Collections
11. Var keyword (Java 10)
12. File APIs
13. Switch expressions (Java 14)
14. Text blocks (Java 15)
15. Records (Java 16)
16. Logging: Log4j
17. Debugging in IDE

Streams provide declarative, pipeline-based data processing.
Does NOT modify the original collection. Lazy evaluation.

STREAM PIPELINE:
  Source → Intermediate ops (lazy) → Terminal op (triggers execution)

CREATING STREAMS:
  list.stream()                   // from Collection
  Arrays.stream(array)            // from array
  Stream.of("a", "b", "c")        // from values
  IntStream.range(1, 10)          // range
  Files.lines(path)               // from file

INTERMEDIATE OPERATIONS (return Stream — lazy):
  .filter(Predicate)              // keep matching elements
  .map(Function)                  // transform each element
  .flatMap(Function)              // flatten nested streams
  .sorted()                       // natural order
  .sorted(Comparator)             // custom order
  .distinct()                     // remove duplicates
  .limit(n)                       // first n elements
  .skip(n)                        // skip first n
  .peek(Consumer)                 // debug — doesn't consume

TERMINAL OPERATIONS (trigger execution):
  .collect(Collectors.toList())   // collect to List
  .collect(Collectors.toMap(...)) // collect to Map
  .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Employee::getDept)) // group
  .forEach(Consumer)              // iterate
  .count()                        // number of elements
  .findFirst()                    // Optional<T>
  .anyMatch(Predicate)            // boolean
  .allMatch(Predicate)            // boolean
  .reduce(0, Integer::sum)        // fold to single value
  .toList()                       // Java 16+

EXAMPLES:
  // Names of employees in IT dept, sorted, distinct
  List<String> names = employees.stream()
    .filter(e -> e.getDept().equals("IT"))
    .map(Employee::getName)
    .sorted()
    .distinct()
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

  // Average salary by department
  Map<String, Double> avgByDept = employees.stream()
    .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(
      Employee::getDept,
      Collectors.averagingDouble(Employee::getSalary)
    ));

PARALLEL STREAMS:
  list.parallelStream()  // uses ForkJoinPool — use only for CPU-bound, large data

text: Java 9-17 Features (Records, var, Text Blocks, Switch)

description:
Week-3 of the HCL Java CDC program covers Web Applications & Services.

Sections:
1. Web Applications & Services (6 Hours)
2. Version Control - Git (3 Hours)
3. Basic of DevOps (4 Hours)
4. Cloud Computing Services (3 Hours)
5. Capstone and Hacker Assessment

Faculty:
• Web Apps: Dr Rajkumar S — rajkumar.s@vit.ac.in
• Git: Dr. Srisakthi Saravanan
• Cloud: Dr Priyaadharshini M

Goal: Understand microservices architecture, REST, DevOps toolchain, and Cloud deployment.

WEB APPLICATION ARCHITECTURE:

LAYERED ARCHITECTURE:
  Presentation Layer (React frontend)
  ↕ HTTP/REST
  API Gateway
  ↕
  Business Logic Layer (Spring Boot services)
  ↕
  Data Access Layer (JPA/Hibernate)
  ↕
  Database (MySQL / MongoDB)

MONOLITHIC vs MICROSERVICES:

Monolith:
  • Single deployable unit — all features in one app
  • Simple to develop and test initially
  • Hard to scale individual features
  • One bug can bring down entire app
  • Suited for small teams, early-stage products

Microservices:
  • Each feature is an independent service (user-service, order-service, payment-service)
  • Independent deployment and scaling
  • Each service owns its database
  • Services communicate via REST or messaging (Kafka, RabbitMQ)
  • Suited for large teams, high-scale systems

MICROSERVICES WITH SPRING BOOT:
  Service Registry & Discovery (Eureka):
    All services register with Eureka on startup
    Client services query Eureka to find server address
    @EnableEurekaServer (registry) and @EnableDiscoveryClient (each service)

  API Gateway (Spring Cloud Gateway):
    Single entry point for all clients
    Routes: /api/users/** → user-service, /api/orders/** → order-service
    Cross-cutting: auth, rate limiting, logging, CORS

  Load Balancing:
    Multiple instances of same service
    Gateway distributes requests (round-robin by default)

  Communication:
    Synchronous: REST via OpenFeign (declarative HTTP client)
    Asynchronous: RabbitMQ / Kafka messages (decoupled)

INTRO TO REST:
  HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS, HEAD
  Status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request,
    401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 500 Internal Server Error
  Richardson Maturity Model: Level 0 → Level 3 (HATEOAS)

BLOCKING vs NON-BLOCKING:
  Blocking (Servlet): thread per request, waits for I/O
  Non-blocking (Reactive/WebFlux): event loop, does not block thread on I/O
  Use WebFlux for high-concurrency, streaming, or event-driven use cases

Instructor-led session — Duration: 3 Hours
Faculty: Dr. Srisakthi Saravanan

GIT BASICS:
  Git is a distributed version control system.
  Every developer has a full copy of the repo (unlike SVN which is centralised).

KEY CONCEPTS:
  Working Directory → Staging Area → Local Repo → Remote Repo
  git add     — stage changes
  git commit  — save staged changes to local repo
  git push    — upload to remote (GitHub/GitLab)
  git pull    — fetch + merge from remote
  git fetch   — download without merging

VERSIONING & STAGING:
  git init                       # new repo
  git clone <url>                # copy remote repo
  git status                     # what changed?
  git diff                       # unstaged changes
  git diff --staged              # staged changes
  git add file.txt               # stage specific file
  git add .                      # stage all changes
  git commit -m "feat: add user login"
  git log --oneline --graph      # pretty history

BRANCHING:
  git branch feature/login       # create branch
  git checkout feature/login     # switch
  git checkout -b feature/login  # create + switch (shortcut)
  git switch -c feature/login    # modern syntax

MERGING vs REBASE:
  git merge feature/login        # merge commit — preserves history
  git rebase main                # reapply commits on top of main — clean linear history
  Prefer merge for shared branches, rebase for local cleanup

ROLLBACK:
  git revert <commit>            # safe — creates new commit undoing changes
  git reset --soft HEAD~1        # undo commit, keep changes staged
  git reset --hard HEAD~1        # DESTRUCTIVE — undo commit AND discard changes
  git stash                      # temporarily shelve changes
  git stash pop                  # restore stashed changes

GIT SSH:
  ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "email@example.com"
  cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub      # copy to GitHub Settings → SSH Keys
  ssh -T git@github.com          # test connection

COMMIT CONVENTIONS:
  feat: new feature | fix: bug fix | docs: documentation
  refactor: code restructure | test: add tests | chore: tooling

Mentor-led session — Duration: 4 Hours

MAVEN / GRADLE (Build Automation):
  Maven uses pom.xml. Gradle uses build.gradle.
  Phases: validate → compile → test → package → install → deploy
  mvn clean install    — compile + test + create JAR
  mvn spring-boot:run  — run Spring Boot app
  Manages dependencies (like npm for Java)

DOCKER & CONTAINERIZATION:
  Container = lightweight, isolated process with its own filesystem.
  Image = blueprint for a container (layers of filesystem changes)

  # Dockerfile for Spring Boot
  FROM eclipse-temurin:17-jdk-alpine
  COPY target/app.jar app.jar
  ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"]

  docker build -t my-app:1.0 .       # build image
  docker run -p 8080:8080 my-app:1.0 # run container
  docker ps                           # running containers
  docker-compose up                   # multi-container (app + db)

  Docker Compose example:
  services:
    app:
      build: .
      ports: ["8080:8080"]
      depends_on: [db]
    db:
      image: mysql:8
      environment:
        MYSQL_DATABASE: mydb
        MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: root

KUBERNETES (K8s) INTRODUCTION:
  Orchestrates containers at scale.
  Pod: smallest unit — one or more containers
  Deployment: manages replicas of pods (rolling updates, rollbacks)
  Service: stable DNS name + load balancing across pods
  Ingress: HTTP routing to services
  kubectl get pods — list running pods
  kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml — apply config

CI/CD PIPELINES:
  CI = Continuous Integration: auto build + test on every commit
  CD = Continuous Deployment: auto deploy to production

  GitHub Actions example (.github/workflows/build.yml):
  on: push
  jobs:
    build:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      steps:
        - uses: actions/checkout@v3
        - uses: actions/setup-java@v3
          with: { java-version: '17' }
        - run: mvn clean install
        - run: docker build -t my-app .
        - run: docker push my-app

  Tools: Jenkins (self-hosted), GitHub Actions, GitLab CI/CD

Mentor-led session — Duration: 3 Hours
Faculty: Dr Priyaadharshini M

CLOUD SERVICE MODELS:
  IaaS — Infrastructure as a Service: raw VMs, storage, networking (AWS EC2, Azure VMs)
  PaaS — Platform as a Service: managed platform, you deploy code (Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine)
  SaaS — Software as a Service: ready to use (Gmail, Salesforce)

TYPES OF CLOUD DEPLOYMENT:
  Public Cloud: AWS, Azure, GCP — shared infrastructure, pay-as-you-go
  Private Cloud: on-premises, company-owned hardware
  Hybrid Cloud: mix of public and private
  Multi-Cloud: using multiple providers to avoid vendor lock-in

KEY CLOUD SERVICES (AWS examples):
  API Gateway    — managed API endpoint, rate limiting, auth
  S3             — object/file storage (like a hard drive in the cloud)
  RDS            — managed relational database (MySQL, PostgreSQL)
  EC2            — virtual machines
  Lambda         — serverless functions (no server management)
  EKS            — managed Kubernetes
  CloudFront     — CDN (content delivery network)

PaaS - INTRODUCTION:
  Deploy your Spring Boot JAR — platform handles servers, scaling, OS patching
  Focus on code, not infrastructure
  Examples: AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Heroku, Railway

ARCHITECTING FOR SCALABILITY & RELIABILITY:
  Horizontal scaling: add more instances (vs vertical = bigger machine)
  Stateless services: no session state → easy to scale
  Database: read replicas for read-heavy workloads
  Caching: Redis between app and database
  Auto-scaling: scale out on high CPU, scale in when idle
  Health checks: load balancer routes only to healthy instances

  Reliability patterns:
  • Circuit Breaker — fail fast, avoid cascade failures
  • Retry with exponential backoff
  • Bulkhead — isolate failures to one component
  • Timeout — always set timeouts on external calls

  Security:
  • IAM roles — least privilege
  • Secrets Manager — never hardcode passwords
  • VPC — private network, restrict internet access
  • WAF — web application firewall

End of Week-3 assessment covering Frontend, Backend, and Web Architecture.

CAPSTONE PROJECT REQUIREMENTS:
• Full-stack application with ReactJS frontend + Spring Boot backend
• RESTful API with proper HTTP methods and status codes
• Database integration (MySQL or MongoDB)
• Authentication with JWT
• Git version control with proper branching
• Containerised with Docker

HACKER ASSESSMENT TOPICS:
• Spring Boot REST API development
• Microservices concepts (architecture questions)
• Git commands and workflows
• Docker commands and Dockerfile
• Cloud concepts (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS, deployment types)
• JWT authentication flow
• Spring Security configuration
• JPA entity relationships

PREP CHECKLIST:
□ Build a complete CRUD REST API with Spring Boot
□ Implement JWT auth from scratch
□ Write Dockerfile for a Spring Boot app
□ Set up docker-compose with app + MySQL
□ Create GitHub Actions workflow for build
□ Deploy to a PaaS (Railway, Heroku, or Render — free tiers)
□ Understand Eureka + API Gateway setup at conceptual level

Week-4 of the HCL Java CDC program covers Database Management.

Sections:
1. Databases — MySQL/SQL (10 Hours)
2. NO-SQL — MongoDB (12 Hours)
3. Authentication & Authorization — JWT (5 Hours)
4. Unit Testing — JUnit + Mockito (5 Hours)

Faculty:
• SQL: Dr. JENILA LIVINGSTON L M — jenila.lm@vit.ac.in
• MongoDB: Dr. LENINISHA SHANMUGAM
• JWT: Dr. MANSOOR HUSSAIN D
• Unit Testing: Dr. ALOK CHAUHAN

Goal: Build and query relational and NoSQL databases, implement JWT-secured APIs, write proper unit tests.

Instructor-led session — Duration: 10 Hours
Faculty: Dr. JENILA LIVINGSTON L M

Relational databases store data in tables (rows and columns) with relationships.
MySQL is an open-source RDBMS — most popular for Spring Boot applications.

MYSQL ENGINES:
  InnoDB (default) — ACID-compliant, foreign keys, transactions, row-level locking
  MyISAM — faster reads, no transactions, table-level locking (legacy)
  MEMORY — in-memory, fastest, data lost on restart

Topics:
• Basic queries — CREATE DATABASE, CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, ALTER
• SELECT query & operations
• COUNT & SUM
• UPDATE & DELETE
• ORDER BY and GROUP BY
• AND, OR, BETWEEN, IN, LIKE
• Joins
• Working with Dates
• Auto Increment
• Triggers
• Index & Views
• Commit & Rollback (transactions)
• Functions — MySQL built-in & User Defined
• SQL Queries — CRUD, Joins, Aggregations
• Database Indexing
• Integration with Spring Boot

Week-5 of the HCL Java CDC program covers Code Quality, Security, and Full-Stack Integration.

Sections:
1. Code Coverage Techniques and Tools (2 Hours)
2. SonarQube Implementation
3. Full-Stack Integration (8 Hours)
4. Security Tools
5. Deployment

Faculty: Dr. SUVIDHA RUPESH KUMAR — suvidha.rupesh@vit.ac.in

Goal: Write testable, high-quality code. Measure and improve code coverage. Connect React frontend to Spring Boot backend. Deploy the complete application.

Duration: 2 Hours

WHAT IS CODE COVERAGE?
Code coverage = percentage of source code lines/branches/paths executed by tests.
High coverage does NOT mean tests are good — but low coverage guarantees gaps.

HOW IS IT MEASURED?
Tools instrument bytecode at compile time to record which lines execute during tests.
Reports show: covered (green), partially covered (yellow), not covered (red).

COVERAGE TYPES:
  Line Coverage     — % of lines executed by tests (most basic)
  Branch Coverage   — % of decision branches (if/else, switch cases) tested
  Method Coverage   — % of methods called
  Class Coverage    — % of classes instantiated
  Instruction Coverage — % of JVM bytecode instructions (most granular)
  Path Coverage     — every possible execution path (exponential — impractical)

CODE COVERAGE vs TEST COVERAGE:
  Code coverage: which lines ran
  Test coverage: which requirements/scenarios are tested
  A line can be executed without being properly asserted → 100% coverage, 0% confidence

COVERAGE TARGETS:
  Industry standard: 70-80% for production code
  Don't chase 100%: getters, setters, boilerplate, config classes are low value
  Focus on: business logic, edge cases, error paths

COVERAGE TOOLS:
  JaCoCo — Java Code Coverage (most popular for Spring Boot)
    Add to pom.xml: jacoco-maven-plugin
    mvn test jacoco:report → generates HTML report in target/site/jacoco/

  SonarQube — integrates JaCoCo, adds code quality analysis
  IntelliJ IDEA — built-in coverage runner (Run with Coverage)
  Cobertura — legacy Java coverage tool
  Istanbul/nyc — JavaScript/Node.js coverage
  pytest-cov — Python coverage

Faculty: Dr. SUVIDHA RUPESH KUMAR

WHAT IS SONARQUBE?
Static code analysis platform — finds bugs, vulnerabilities, code smells, and duplications before they reach production.

SONARQUBE METRICS:
  Bugs            — actual or likely runtime errors
  Vulnerabilities — security issues (SQL injection, XSS, hardcoded passwords)
  Code Smells     — maintainability issues (long methods, dead code, complex logic)
  Duplications    — copy-pasted code blocks
  Technical Debt  — estimated time to fix all issues
  Security Hotspots — code to review for security
  Coverage        — from JaCoCo integration

SONARQUBE ARCHITECTURE:
  SonarQube Server (web, compute engine, search)
  Database (stores analysis results)
  SonarScanner (runs in CI, sends results to server)

DOCKERIZED SETUP:
  docker run -d --name sonarqube \
    -p 9000:9000 \
    sonarqube:latest
  # Access: http://localhost:9000 (admin/admin)

MAVEN PROJECT SCANNING:
  // pom.xml — add sonar properties
  <properties>
    <sonar.projectKey>my-project</sonar.projectKey>
    <sonar.host.url>http://localhost:9000</sonar.host.url>
    <sonar.login>your-token</sonar.login>
  </properties>

  mvn clean verify sonar:sonar  // scan + report

SONARQUBE QUALITY GATE:
  Pass/Fail threshold for:
  • Coverage > 80%
  • No new critical bugs
  • No new vulnerabilities
  • Duplication < 3%
  Integrate with CI — fail build if Quality Gate fails

NODE.JS / FRONTEND SCANNING:
  sonar-scanner -Dsonar.projectKey=react-app \
    -Dsonar.sources=src \
    -Dsonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths=coverage/lcov.info

SSL SETUP:
  Generate SSL cert → configure nginx reverse proxy → sonarqube behind HTTPS
  Used in production environments

TRIVY — Container & Filesystem Vulnerability Scanner:
  Scans Docker images, filesystems, and Git repos for:
  • CVEs in OS packages and application dependencies
  • Misconfigurations (Dockerfile, Kubernetes YAML)
  • Exposed secrets

  trivy image openjdk:17                    # scan Docker image
  trivy fs ./                               # scan filesystem
  trivy repo https://github.com/org/repo   # scan git repo

  Integrate in CI:
  - name: Trivy scan
    run: trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL my-app:latest
  # Fails build if CRITICAL vulnerability found

OWASP DEPENDENCY-CHECK:
  Scans Java (Maven/Gradle) and Node.js dependencies for known CVEs.
  OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities to know:
    A01: Broken Access Control
    A02: Cryptographic Failures
    A03: Injection (SQL, LDAP, XSS)
    A07: Identification/Authentication Failures
    A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures

  Maven integration:
  <plugin>
    <groupId>org.owasp</groupId>
    <artifactId>dependency-check-maven</artifactId>
    <version>9.0.0</version>
  </plugin>
  mvn dependency-check:check

PROWLER — Cloud Security Tool:
  Open-source cloud security assessment tool for AWS, Azure, GCP.
  Checks: IAM policies, S3 bucket permissions, security groups, encryption.
  prowler aws --profile default --region ap-south-1
  Generates HTML report with findings by severity.

DOCKLE — Docker Image Linter:
  Checks Dockerfile for security best practices:
  • Running as non-root user
  • Removing shell
  • Using specific image versions (not :latest)
  • Minimising layer count
  dockle my-app:latest

DEPLOYMENT STRATEGY FOR FULL-STACK JAVA APP:

FRONTEND (React):
  Build: npm run build  → dist/ folder (static HTML/CSS/JS)
  Deploy to:
  • Netlify: drag-and-drop or connect GitHub repo — auto-deploys on push
  • Vercel: git push → automatic build + CDN deployment
  • GitHub Pages: for static sites
  • AWS S3 + CloudFront: production-grade CDN

BACKEND (Spring Boot):
  Build: mvn clean package  → target/app.jar
  Deploy to:
  • Railway (free tier): connect GitHub, auto-deploys Spring Boot
  • Render: free tier, Docker or Java support
  • Heroku: easy PaaS, but now paid
  • AWS Elastic Beanstalk: managed PaaS on AWS
  • Docker + VPS: full control

VERSION CONTROL WORKFLOW:
  main branch — production code only
  develop branch — integration branch
  feature/login — feature branches
  Flow: feature → PR → code review → merge to develop → test → merge to main → deploy

MINI PROJECT DEPLOYMENT CHECKLIST:
  □ Frontend: npm run build, deploy to Netlify
  □ Backend: jar deployed to Railway/Render
  □ Database: Railway MySQL or PlanetScale
  □ Environment variables: never commit secrets
  □ CORS: production frontend URL whitelisted in Spring Boot
  □ JWT secret: stored in environment variable, not code
  □ GitHub Actions: auto-deploy on push to main

SAMPLE GITHUB ACTIONS — Full Deploy:
  jobs:
    deploy-backend:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      steps:
        - mvn clean package
        - docker build + push to Docker Hub
        - SSH deploy to server (or Railway webhook)
    deploy-frontend:
      - npm run build
      - netlify deploy --prod --dir=dist

Week-6 of the HCL Java CDC program covers AI-assisted development with GitHub Copilot.
Faculty: Dr. VATCHALA S — vatchala.s@vit.ac.in

GenAI tools like GitHub Copilot are now standard in enterprise software development.
HCL engineers are expected to use Copilot productively and responsibly.

Topics:
1. GitHub Copilot Overview
2. Basic Copilot Usage
3. Understanding Copilot Suggestions
4. Generative Commit Messages
5. Understanding Copilot Chat
6. Copilot for the CLI
7. Copilot's Limitations and Strengths
8. Troubleshooting and Common Issues
9. GitHub Copilot Language Support
10. Code Documentation with Copilot
11. Copilot in IDEs (VS Code, IntelliJ)
12. Code Optimization with Copilot
13. Refactoring Strategies
14. NLP and GitHub Copilot
15. Debugging with Copilot
16. Copilot Best Practices
17. Usage in existing/legacy code
18. Final Assessment

WHAT IS GITHUB COPILOT?
AI pair programmer powered by OpenAI Codex/GPT-4.
Trained on billions of lines of public code from GitHub.
Integrated directly into your IDE — suggests code as you type.

HOW IT WORKS:
  You type a comment or partial code → Copilot predicts the completion.
  It uses context from: current file, open files, function signatures, variable names, comments.

INSTALLATION:
  VS Code: Extensions → search "GitHub Copilot" → Install
  IntelliJ: Plugins → search "GitHub Copilot" → Install
  Requires GitHub account with Copilot subscription
  (Free for students via GitHub Student Developer Pack)

BASIC USAGE:
  // Write a comment describing what you want
  // Function to validate email address
  // → Copilot suggests the full function

  // Start typing and Copilot completes
  public List<User> findByAge(  // → Copilot suggests params + body

  // Accept suggestion: Tab
  // Next suggestion: Alt+]
  // Previous suggestion: Alt+[
  // Dismiss: Escape
  // Open all suggestions: Ctrl+Enter

COPILOT CHAT:
  Separate chat panel — ask questions about code, request refactoring.
  /explain — explain selected code
  /fix     — fix a bug in selection
  /tests   — generate unit tests
  /docs    — generate documentation
  Ask: "What does this method do?", "How can I optimise this query?"

COPILOT FOR CLI:
  gh copilot suggest "delete all docker containers"  # suggests shell command
  gh copilot explain "kubectl get pods -n kube-system"  # explains command

GENERATIVE COMMIT MESSAGES:
  In VS Code: Stage changes → Source Control panel → sparkle icon → generates commit message
  Based on diff content — descriptive, follows conventional commits format

text: Effective Copilot Use — Suggestions, Optimization, Refactoring
description:
UNDERSTANDING COPILOT SUGGESTIONS:
  Copilot is a probabilistic model — suggestions are its BEST GUESS, not ground truth.
  Always review suggestions critically:
  • Does it do what the comment says?
  • Are there edge cases it missed?
  • Could it introduce a security vulnerability?
  • Is the suggested API current (not deprecated)?

GETTING BETTER SUGGESTIONS:
  • Write descriptive comments: // Validate that email follows RFC 5322 format, return error message if invalid
  • Use meaningful variable names: userEmailAddress not uea
  • Keep related code open in adjacent tabs — Copilot uses them as context
  • Write the function signature first, then let Copilot complete the body
  • For Spring Boot: name controller methods descriptively — Copilot infers intent

CODE OPTIMIZATION WITH COPILOT:
  Select slow code → Copilot Chat → "Optimise this for performance"
  "Use Java Streams instead of this for loop"
  "Replace this O(n²) with O(n log n)"
  "Use StringBuilder instead of String concatenation in loop"

REFACTORING STRATEGIES:
  Select method → /fix or ask "Refactor to follow SOLID principles"
  "Extract this logic into a separate method"
  "Convert this class to use the Builder pattern"
  "Replace if-else chain with Strategy pattern"
  "Make this thread-safe"

CODE DOCUMENTATION:
  Select method → /docs → generates Javadoc
  Or write /** and Copilot completes the Javadoc
  Generates @param, @return, @throws automatically

DEBUGGING WITH COPILOT:
  Paste stack trace → "What's causing this NullPointerException?"
  Select failing test → "Why is this test failing?"
  "Add error handling to this method"
  "What edge cases am I missing?"

NLP AND COPILOT:
  Copilot understands natural language in comments.
  Write requirements in plain English → get code.
  // Parse a CSV file with headers on first line, return list of maps where keys are header names
  // Handle missing fields and skip blank lines
  → Copilot generates the full parser

text: Copilot Limitations, Best Practices & Legacy Code
description:
COPILOT'S LIMITATIONS:
  • Hallucinations: may suggest APIs that don't exist or have wrong signatures
  • Outdated knowledge: may suggest deprecated methods or older library versions
  • Security blind spots: can reproduce insecure patterns from training data
  • No runtime awareness: doesn't know your actual data or system state
  • Context window: only sees part of your codebase
  • Not always correct: acceptance rate in production teams is ~30-40%

COPILOT'S STRENGTHS:
  • Boilerplate reduction: DTOs, getters/setters, test stubs, CRUD methods
  • Pattern completion: recognises Spring, JPA, React patterns
  • Documentation generation: Javadoc, README sections
  • Language polyglot: switches between Java, SQL, YAML, JavaScript seamlessly
  • Learning tool: shows you how to use unfamiliar APIs

TROUBLESHOOTING:
  No suggestions appearing:
  • Check Copilot status in IDE status bar (not showing red/orange)
  • Ensure file type is supported
  • Check GitHub Copilot subscription is active
  • Try: Ctrl+Shift+P → "GitHub Copilot: Enable"
  Poor suggestions:
  • Add more context (comments, better naming)
  • Close unrelated files
  • Break the problem into smaller pieces

LANGUAGE SUPPORT:
  Best: Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, C#, Go, C++
  Good: SQL, Kotlin, Swift, Ruby, PHP, Rust
  Limited: niche languages, proprietary DSLs

BEST PRACTICES:
  1. Treat Copilot as a junior developer — always review its code
  2. Don't accept suggestions blindly — understand what they do
  3. Run tests after accepting — Copilot can introduce bugs
  4. Use for boilerplate, not business logic decisions
  5. Don't paste confidential code — it goes to OpenAI servers
  6. Keep Copilot updated — model improves regularly

LEGACY CODE:
  Select legacy method → "Explain what this does"
  "Modernise this Java 6 code to Java 17"
  "Add unit tests for this untested method"
  "Replace anonymous inner classes with lambdas"
  "Convert this XML Spring config to Java @Configuration"
  Copilot is particularly useful for understanding old, uncommented code.

FINAL ASSESSMENT TOPICS:
  • Copilot keyboard shortcuts
  • When to trust vs review suggestions
  • Using /explain, /fix, /tests commands
  • CLI usage with gh copilot
  • Responsible AI usage policies
